generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the wind howling like a beast at the door, the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The lantern is lit, as it must be, and the light will guide the ships through the darkness, as it has for years. But tonight, I fear it may not be enough.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I suppose you think me a monster, a man who locked you out of his heart the moment you stepped through that door. But I beg you to understand—there was no choice. The sea is cruel, and I could not bear to see you cast adrift upon its tides. You were always the shore to me, and I feared that if I let you go, you would be lost.

I have not slept in days, watching the storm gather strength. The waves are rising, and the wind is fierce. I have no doubt that the light will hold, but I do not know if I will. If this night takes me, I hope you will remember that I loved you, and that I always have.

Do not come back. I cannot bear to see you again.

With all the love that remains in my heart,

Your father

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**  
**To My Dearest Eliza**  
**From Your Father, William Hargrave**  
**Lighthouse of St. Agnes**  
**14th of November, 1878**  

**My Dearest Eliza,**  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. These words are not meant for the world to see, but I must send them, for I fear the storm that looms upon us shall claim me before I can speak to you in person. If this letter finds you, may it bring you some comfort, though I know not if it will bring you peace.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not heard from you in all that time. I have written to you, but your replies—when they came at all—were brief and cold, as though you could not bear to say the words that lay between us. I know you are not unkind, but I fear the distance we have placed between us has grown too great for words to mend.

I have always believed that a father's duty is to protect his child, and I have done my best to do so. Yet I fear I have failed you in more ways than one. I was a man of duty, of routine, of the sea and the light. I was not a man of sentiment, and I did not understand that love must be shown, not just felt.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken between us, but with the weight of a storm that threatens to swallow the world—and with the knowledge that time is running out. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have not forgotten you, I have grown weary of the silence. The sea has always been my companion, my guide, my burden, and now, as the night falls and the storm approaches, I find myself wondering if it is the sea or the silence that will claim me first.

You were once my greatest joy, and yet, in the years since you left, I have found myself adrift in a sea of regret and longing. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that by the time you receive this letter, I may be gone. But I write not to beg for your forgiveness, for I know that time has long since passed, and that words cannot undo what has been done. I write instead to tell you the truth—about the storm, about the lighthouse, about the life I have led, and about the love I have carried for you all these years.

The storm is unlike any I have seen. The skies have darkened with a fury that speaks of something ancient and unrelenting. The wind howls like a beast, and the waves rise like mountains, crashing against the cliffs with a force that defies reason. I have

*The lighthouse, 1892*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that feels as though it might shatter beneath the weight of the wind. The sea howls like a beast in pain, and the lantern’s light flickers—fitful, defiant, like me.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, that cold, empty house where I still keep your childhood drawings tucked beneath the floorboards. I’ve not spoken your name aloud in all that time, not even to the sea. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the waves grow louder, I feel the need to speak. To tell you what I’ve kept locked away in the deepest chamber of my soul.

You were a child when you ran from me, as children do. Or so I told myself. But I know now that you were not running from me, but from the truth. The truth that I am not the man I once was. That the fire in my chest has long since turned to ash. That I have spent these years watching the sea, not for the stars, but for the silence.

You used to come to the lighthouse when you were little. You’d sit on the steps and ask me if the light could see the stars. I’d tell you yes, and that the light never slept. You believed me. I let you believe me

**My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the kind of letter you were hoping to receive. I’ve written it with a heavy heart, and my hands tremble as I hold the pen. It’s been five long years since we last spoke, and I have not been able to find the right words to mend what has been broken between us. Tonight, as I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, I find myself thinking of you more than I ever have before.

The storm is coming. It has been gathering strength all day, a monstrous thing of wind and fury, and I can feel its breath against the cliffs. I have spent my life watching the sea, and I know that when the waves rise like that, they do not stop. They do not ask for mercy. They do not listen. And I do not know if I will make it through this night.

I have not told you this before, but I have always felt a great responsibility to protect those who cannot protect themselves. That is what I have done all these years—stood guard over the sea, guiding ships through the darkness, ensuring that none are lost to the merciless tide. But I have never been able to protect you from the things that have hurt you, and for that, I am truly sorry.

I know you think I have abandoned you. That I have chosen the sea over you. But I have never stopped thinking of

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in the best of health and spirits, though I fear it may not find you at all, for I write this with the weight of a storm upon me, and the sea in its wrath. The winds howl like the very spirits of the dead, and the waves crash against the rocks with such fury that I fear for the very foundations of this lighthouse. I am not a man of great words, but I must write, for I know that if I do not, I may never speak to you again.

It has been five long years since we last met, and I have long since ceased to hope that you will ever return. Yet, as I write these lines, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. It is strange, for I have not seen you since you left, and I have not heard from you since the day you left the house in the spring of 1868. I remember that day as if it were yesterday. You were in a great hurry, your eyes filled with anger and your heart filled with sorrow. You said little, and I said less, for I feared that to speak would only bring more pain.

You were my only child, Eleanor, and I have always loved you more than life itself. Yet I fear that in my stubbornness and my pride, I have done you great wrong. I have not written to you, nor have I sent

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**In the Name of the Lord and the Sea, I do write this, though my hands tremble and my breath comes short.**

It is near upon midnight, and the wind howls like the very devil hath taken root in the rocks of this bleak coast. The waves, they rise with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The lantern, which I have tended with the care of a mother’s hand, is but a frail thing against such a tempest. I fear, my child, that I may not see the dawn.

But as I sit here, the lantern’s glow flickering like a soul in torment, I find myself thinking of you, and of the days that were once ours—before the sea took its toll on our hearts.

I have not heard from you in five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I have written to you, I have begged you to answer, but you have given me no word. I took it as a sign that you had turned your back on me, as you did on your father. Yet now, in this hour of darkness, I find myself wondering if it is not the sea that has taken your letters, or if it is the very devil himself who has made you blind to the truth of our kinship.

I have raised you, my dear, with the same care that I have tended this lantern. I have taught you the ways of the

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it may find you in a state of disbelief. For I write to you now from the very cliffs that have stood guard over our village for generations, and I do so with a heart heavy with sorrow and longing.

Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and yet the weight of that day still clings to me like the salt air that stings my eyes at dawn. I have not spoken to you, nor have I seen you, and I have come to think that perhaps I have not been a father as I should have been. You are no longer the little girl who used to run up the hill to watch the lighthouse glow, but a woman with her own path, her own heart, and her own burdens.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a letter, for I feared what I might say. And yet, as the storm gathers strength and the sea grows restless, I find myself with a voice I have not used in years, and a heart that aches to speak.

You were right, Eleanor, when you told me that I was too stern, too distant. I was a lighthouse keeper, and I thought that the light I gave to the sailors was the only light I could give to my children. But I was wrong. You were not a sailor, and I was not a beacon. I was

**[The letter is written on coarse, yellowed paper, the ink smudged from damp fingers. The margins are uneven, as though the writer was too hurried or too distressed to care.]**

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a beast at my door, and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the land whole. The storm comes with a fury I have not seen in many a year, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn.

You were but a child when you left, and I did not stop you. I did not know then what I know now—that you carried a heart that could not abide the weight of a father’s silence. I have been a man of stone, Eleanor, and you were the only thing that ever made me feel less so. I would have given you the world, if only you had asked. But you did not.

I have kept the light burning these many years, not for the sea, not for the ships, but for you. I thought, perhaps, that if I kept the light steady, you would one day return. That if I stood guard over this lonely place, you would come to see the stars again, or the dawn, or the way the sea sings at night. But you did not. And I have not written, for

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, by the flickering light of the lighthouse’s lamp, as the wind howls like a banshee through the rocks. The sea roars with fury, and I fear we may not see dawn. But I must write, for if I do not, I may never have the chance to say what I have kept locked inside for five long, bitter years.

You were seventeen when you left this house, the day you walked out the door and never looked back. I tried to stop you, but you were strong, and you had your reasons. I understand now that I was not the father you needed. I was a man bound by duty, by the sea, by the light. I was not there for you when you needed me. I was too busy watching the horizon, too preoccupied with the lives of strangers to notice the one I had in my own home.

I have not spoken your name in five years, not even in my heart, not even in my dreams. But now, as the storm gathers, I feel the weight of that silence like a stone upon my chest. I have spent every night since your departure staring into the fire, wondering if I would ever be able to look at you again, if I would ever be able to say I was sorry.

You were not the only one who suffered. I, too,

**The Lighthouse at Blackmoor Point**  
*April 12th, 1893*  

My Dearest Elara,  

It has been five long years since I last saw you, and though I write this with a steady hand, my heart feels as though it is being tossed upon the waves of a storm I cannot see. I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I have not yet decided whether to send it or not—though I know I must, for there are things I have not said, and I fear I may never have the chance to say them again.  

The storm is coming, Elara. Not just any storm, but one that has been building for weeks, gathering strength with every passing day. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, has turned against us. I’ve been watching the skies all evening, and the clouds are thick as lead, rolling in from the west like a wall of blackened fury. The lighthouse is lit, of course—it always is. But I wonder if it will be enough to keep us both safe.  

I have not spoken to you in years, and I know I have not been the father you deserved. I was too stubborn, too proud, too caught up in the weight of duty and the silence between us. I should have come to you when you needed me,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, and the sea roiling as if it knows the end is near. The storm is coming—violent, unrelenting, and I fear it may take more than just the light from this tower.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and I’ve spent them counting the hours, the days, the nights, all in silence. I’ve whispered your name into the wind, hoping it would carry it to you, but I know you’ve never heard it. You’ve turned your back on me, just as the world has turned its back on this old lighthouse and the man who guards it.

I’ve always been a man of few words, Eleanor. But tonight, I feel the weight of silence more than ever. The sea has always spoken to me—its roar, its whisper, its warnings—but tonight, it speaks in a language only you can understand. I’m not sure if I’ll survive this storm, but if I do, I hope you’ll hear this: I’ve never stopped thinking of you.

You were my light once, my guiding star in a world that grew too dark. I raised you with love, with patience, with all the hope I had. But you left, and I never stopped searching for you. I’ve watched the tides come and go, the stars rise and fall, and I’ve prayed

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may come too late. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it seeks to reclaim the shore. I have spent the day preparing the lighthouse, securing the lamps, and ensuring that the light shall not falter. It is my duty, as it has been for many years, and I have always taken it upon myself with the utmost care.

I have not written to you in five years, and I know I have not been the father you deserved. I have been a man of habit, of duty, of solitude. The sea has claimed much of my time, and I have not been there for you as I should have. I regret that deeply, and I pray that you will forgive me.

Tonight, as the storm approaches with a fury I have never seen, I find myself thinking of you. I have no illusions of survival, not in this tempest. But if I should not make it through the night, I would not wish to leave you with unanswered questions. I have always loved you, Eleanor, more than I can ever express. I have watched over you from afar, and I have prayed for your happiness.

I do not know where you are, or what path you have taken, but I hope you are safe. I hope you have found peace, and that you have not been burdened by the silence between us. I have no

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**  
**To My Dearest Eleanor**  
**From Your Father, Thomas R. Whitmore**  
**Hawthorn Lighthouse**  
**Hawthorn Cove, Devonshire**  
**23rd of April, 1889**  

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I know you will not read these words. I have not seen you in five long years, and though I have written to you many times, I fear they have never reached you. If they have, I pray you do not take this letter as an admission of guilt, for I have tried, in my own way, to reach you.

I am writing this from the lighthouse, as the storm that has been gathering for days now is upon us. The winds howl like wolves, and the sea, in its fury, threatens to swallow the very rocks on which this lighthouse stands. I have lit the beacon, as I always have, and though I know it may not be seen from the mainland, I have done so with the hope that, should you still be watching the skies, you might see its light and know that your father is still here, still standing, still trying.

I do not know if I will survive the night, but if I do, I pray that you will read this. I have no words to apologize for the pain I have caused

Certainly. Here's a letter from a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper to his estranged daughter, written with the weight of regret, longing, and the quiet strength of a man who has seen the sea and the soul.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the hope that these words may reach you in time, even if only in memory. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not spoken to you since. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you have forgotten the man I once was. But I write to you now, not as a father, but as a man who has lived through storms and silence, and who now finds himself facing the greatest storm of all.

Tonight, a terrible storm is coming. The sea is restless, the winds howl like wolves, and the sky is dark with the promise of wrath. I have seen many storms in my time, but none like this. I fear I may not survive it. If I do not, I want you to know that I have not forgotten you, and that I have not stopped loving you.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. I know that time has passed, and that perhaps you have moved on. I know that I have not been there for you when you needed me most. I was a man who was too proud, too stubborn

*[The letter is written in a thick, ink-stained hand, the paper yellowed and curled at the edges. The ink is smudged in places, as though the writer had been shaking or weeping when they wrote it.]*

**April 3rd, 1887**  
**Lighthouse of Blackmoor Point**  
**Northumberland Coast**  

*My Dearest Eleanor,*  

If you are reading this, then I have not survived the storm. Or perhaps I have, and you have come to visit, as you once did when you were a child. Either way, I write this with a hand that trembles, and a heart that is not so sure it can bear the weight of what I must say.

Five years. Five years since you left. Five years since you told me you would not return. Five years of staring out at the sea, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and wondering if the tide would ever bring you back.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not a word. Not a whisper. Not even the sound of your name on my lips without it turning to ash. But tonight... tonight the wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it knows the truth. And I feel it in my bones—this storm is not like the others. It is *wrong*. It is *wrong* in a way that makes my soul shiver.

I

My Dearest Clara,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not be the most fitting time to write. The wind howls outside like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that makes me feel small and fragile. I’ve been watching the waves crash against the cliffs for hours, and I can’t help but think of you—how far away you are, and how close you once were.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I’ve been trying to forget the way your voice sounded, cold and distant, as if I were a stranger to you. I’ve tried to convince myself that you were just angry, that it was all a misunderstanding. But I fear that was never the case. I fear that somewhere in the silence between our words, you stopped listening.

I’ve always tried to be the best father I could be, but I know I failed you. I know I let the work of this lighthouse consume me, that I let the sea take precedence over the life I had with you. I never meant to be the man who was always away, always lost in the light. I never meant to be the one you turned your back on.

Tonight, though, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. The storm is coming, and I don’t know if I’ll make it through. I’ve seen the sky turn black with thunder, and I feel the weight of the world pressing down on

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart full of sorrow and a mind heavy with the weight of years that I take up my quill to write to you once more. It has been five long years since our last correspondence, and in that time, I have watched the tides of the sea and the stars above with aching eyes, ever wondering when I might see you again. The sea, though vast and cruel, is a faithful companion, and so too is the memory of you.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The winds howl like the wails of the damned, and the sky is a canvas of black and fury. The lighthouse, which has stood for generations, stands strong, but I fear for my own life. The waves have grown fierce, and the lantern's light may be extinguished before dawn. Should I not survive this night, I would leave this world with the hope that you may one day read these words.

I have often pondered the reasons for our estrangement, my dear Eleanor. I have not spoken with you in five years, nor have I heard of your life or your family. I do not know if you have wed or if you have children of your own. I have heard whispers in the village that you have gone to London and perhaps even beyond. I have not sought you out, for I feared that my words would only bring more pain. But now, as the

Ah, my dear Eliza, if you be reading these words, then I must be on my last legs, and the sea, in all its cruel and capricious moods, has taken its toll upon me. But I write with the hand of a man who has long since given up the ghost, and with the heart of a father who has long since given up the hope of seeing you again. Yet, in the depths of my soul, I feel the stirrings of a strange and bitter peace, for I have lived long enough to see the stars, and I have kept the light burning for many a soul who might otherwise have perished in the dark.

It is now well into the night, and the wind howls like the very devil himself, as if to mock the feeble efforts of man to stand against the might of nature. The sea has risen in fury, and I fear that the storm which now rages upon us may well be the end of me. Yet I have done my duty, as I have always done, and I shall not have the light go out upon this night, for I have seen the look in the eyes of those poor souls who have come to my door, and I know that the light must not be extinguished, no matter the cost.

I have not seen you in five long years, my child, and I know that I have done wrong in my silence. I was a fool to think that I could keep the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that restless beast, roars as if in fury. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. And yet, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than ever.

You were but a child when you left, and I, a fool, allowed you to go. I should have stood firm, as any father would, and not let you walk out of this house like a ghost. I have spent these years in silence, haunted by the thought that I may have lost not only my daughter, but my soul.

The lighthouse stands tall, as it has for many a year, and the light burns bright, guiding the lost and the weary. I have kept it burning, even when the world turned its back on me. I tell myself it is for the sailors, for the men and women who brave the sea, but I know it is for you as well. For the day you may return, or for the day I may find the strength to reach out again.

The storm is upon us now, and I have lit the lamp. It will burn through the night, and I will stand by the light, as I have done for so many years. I have no fear of the tempest, for I have faced worse than

**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the lighthouse crest]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a beast in the night, the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The light is still burning, as it has for years, but I fear it may be the last time it shines.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I have not spoken your name in that time, nor have I written a single word to you. I do not know if you still remember me, or if you have forgotten the man who once held you close in his arms.

I have not forgotten you. Not a single day. I have watched the waves, and the stars, and the endless sky, and I have thought of you more than I have thought of anything else. I have kept the light burning for you, as I did when you were small, when you would run up the steps and press your face against the glass, and I would smile, and say nothing.

But you did not come back. Not once. Not when I was sick, not when I was alone, not when I needed you most.

I have tried to be strong. To be the man you once knew. But the sea is cruel, and the years have worn me down. I have no more strength left to keep the light alight, and I fear the storm that comes tonight may take it from me

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 1st, 1897**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these stone walls, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very sky. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see morning. But if I do, I must speak these words to you—words that have lain heavy on my heart for five long years.

You were my light, Eleanor. The one thing that kept me from drowning in the silence of this lonely life. I remember the day you left, the way you turned your back to me, as if I were some cursed thing. I tried to reach for you, but you pulled away, and the door closed behind you with a sound like a tomb.

I have not seen you in five years. Five years, and yet it feels like a lifetime. I have tried to forgive you, to understand you, but the wounds you left behind are deeper than any sea. You were my child, my only child, and I gave you the world, and you took it from me.

I have watched the tides rise and fall, the stars blink out one by one, and the storms come and go. Each one has been a reminder of your absence. I have told myself that you would return, that you would come back to the lighthouse, to me. But the days have passed, and still

**The Lighthouse, Cliffs of Dunhaven**  
**October 12th, 1897**

My Dearest Elara,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same old place—wherever that may be. Five years is a long time to go without a word, and I confess, I have often wondered if I should have written before. But perhaps it is too late for that now.

The storm is upon us, and the sea is in a fury. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the rocks with a vengeance. I’ve lit the lamp, of course. It’s my duty, and it’s the only thing that keeps me from going mad. The light has been burning since dusk, and I fear it may outlast us all.

I thought of you this evening as I stood at the window, watching the tempest rage. The sea was once your favorite thing—those endless, rolling waves, the way the moonlight danced on the water. You used to sit on the porch with me, watching the tides, and we’d talk of the future. You spoke of leaving, of chasing some grand dream in the city, of becoming a pianist or a writer. I never said no, though I did wonder if you were too young to know what you wanted.

But I suppose that’s what I’ve always been—too old, too

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From the Edge of the World, Where the Sea Roars and the Sky Weeps**

Five long years have passed since I last held you in my arms, and the silence between us has grown colder than the northern winds that howl through these cliffs. I write this not with the hope that you will read it, but with the certainty that if you do, you will remember who I am—your father, your keeper, the man who once told you the stars were not just light, but promises.

The storm is coming. Not the kind that merely shakes the land, but the kind that cracks the sky and drowns the world in black. The waves are rising like giants, and the lighthouse, my life’s work, stands defiant against the fury of the sea. I have lit the beacon tonight, not just for the sailors who may still be out there, but for you. For the girl who once stood at my feet, wide-eyed and full of questions, and who I failed to answer.

I have no words to apologize for the years I missed, for the silence that grew like a wall between us. I was a fool to think that distance could be a substitute for love. I was a fool to believe that time would mend what was broken. But I have learned, Eleanor, that some wounds do not heal—they fester, and they remain. And I have not been worthy of your forgiveness.

But tonight,

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that brews on the horizon, and with hands that tremble not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since we last exchanged words, and I have often wondered if they were ever truly spoken at all. I have kept the light burning for you, as I have kept it for all who sail these waters, and yet, I have not kept the light of our bond.

You were a child when I last saw you, and now you are a woman, perhaps even a mother. I do not know if you have found peace, or if you have been shaped by the same tempests that have shaped me. I have watched the sea rage and calm, and I have seen the sky turn from blue to black, and still, I have not given up on the hope that one day, you might return.

Tonight, the wind howls like a banshee, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones ache. I have spent my life guarding this light, ensuring that no ship is lost to the dark. But I fear that tonight, the darkness may be too great, and that I may not see the dawn. If I do not, I beg you to remember that I have never stopped watching over you, even in the silence between our words.

Forgive me for the silence, my dear. I have tried,

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From Your Father,  
William Hargrave,  
Keeper of the Light,  
The Sentinel of the Skies,  
Whitecliff Lighthouse,  
Northamptonshire,  
England**

**October 15th, 1884**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The ink upon this page may fade, but the words within shall remain, should I not be found in the morning. Tonight, the skies darken with a fury that has not been seen in these parts for many a year. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that gentle mother to our shores, roars with a voice that shakes the very bones of the earth.

I sit here by the light, the wind howling like a banshee, and I feel the weight of the world on my shoulders. **My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the lamp burning low and the storm at the door. The wind howls as if it knows the truth—knows that I am not the man I once was, and that I may not live to see the dawn.

Five years. Five years since you left. Five years since I last saw your face, your voice, your *presence*. The sea has been kinder than I have been to you, I fear.

I have kept the lighthouse these many years, as I always said I would. The light burns every night, as it must. But tonight, I fear it may be the last. The storm is unlike any I've seen. The waves rise like mountains, and the sky is black as the grave. I’ve seen men die in such tempests, and I have no wish to be among them.

But I cannot stop now. Not while the light still burns. Not while the sea still roars.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**The Lighthouse at Blackmoor Point**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds me far from it. **To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls outside, a sound that has always been both my companion and my torment. Tonight, the storm is coming—wild, unrelenting, and perhaps even crueler than the silence that has kept us apart.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have counted every day, every hour, every breath. I’ve kept the lighthouse burning, not just for the sailors who might be lost in the dark, but for the hope that one day, you might return. I’ve kept the light alive, even when the world felt like it had gone dark.

I know you’ve been angry. I know you’ve blamed me for the choices we made, for the distance that grew between us. But I want you to know this: I never stopped loving you. I never stopped praying for you. Even when the world turned its back on me, I held onto the belief that you were somewhere, safe, and whole. I told myself that one day, when the storms passed and the skies cleared, I would find you again.
